page: Alibaba Cloud ecs.re6p.26xlarge is an x86_64 architecture server instance powered by the KVM hypervisor. It features an Intel Xeon 8269CY processor configured with 104 dedicated physical cores and 104 vCPUs, indicating a one-to-one mapping with no hyperthreading enabled. The system is equipped with 384.0 GB of RAM, providing approximately 3.69 GB of memory per core.

This instance does not include local storage, meaning all storage requirements must be met through external network-attached block storage. Network connectivity is supported by a baseline bandwidth of 25 Gbps. From a qualitative cost-efficiency perspective, the dedicated core allocation ensures predictable performance without resource contention, though the lack of bundled local storage and GPUs requires external provisioning for storage-heavy or acceleration-dependent workloads.

The ecs.re6p.26xlarge is suited for general-purpose workloads requiring high parallel processing capabilities without hyperthreading overhead. Suitable applications include large-scale web serving, enterprise application hosting, distributed computing tasks, and moderate database workloads that rely on network-attached storage.
description: Alibaba Cloud ecs.re6p.26xlarge is an x86_64 server instance running on the KVM hypervisor. It is equipped with an Intel Xeon 8269CY processor featuring 104 dedicated physical cores and 104 vCPUs, providing a dedicated compute environment with no hyperthreading. The instance includes 384.0 GB of RAM, yielding 3.69 GB of memory per core, and offers a baseline network bandwidth of 25 Gbps. It does not contain local storage or GPU hardware, meaning storage must be provisioned externally. This hardware profile makes the instance highly suitable for general-purpose enterprise applications, parallel processing tasks, and web serving workloads that require consistent CPU performance without resource sharing.

tagline: A dedicated multi-core x86_64 compute platform featuring balanced memory allocation and high-bandwidth network connectivity for enterprise workloads.
